---
title: "Evolving Memory Agent Setup"
subtitle: "A Complete Guide to OpenSpace, nanobot, and Local AI"
author: "Ilya Kashnitsky"
date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-fold: false
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: scroll
    highlight-style: github
    embed-resources: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        body { font-size: 0.95rem; }
        h1.title { font-size: 1.8rem; }
        .callout { margin-block: 1.2rem; }
        code { font-size: 0.85em; }
        pre.sourceCode { font-size: 0.85em; }
        </style>
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
---

# Introduction: What Are We Building?

Welcome! This guide will help you build a powerful, self-evolving AI assistant. Because we are combining several tools, it helps to understand the "anatomy" of what we are building:

* **The Interface (Open WebUI & Telegram)**: How you talk to your AI.
* **The Brains (Ollama, Gemini, OpenRouter)**: The engines that process your text. Ollama runs locally; Gemini runs in the cloud.
* **The Hands (MCP)**: Tools that let the AI search the web, read your files, or check its memory.
* **The Agents (OpenSpace & nanobot)**: The "managers." OpenSpace learns how to do complex tasks (like writing R code) and remembers them. nanobot handles your Telegram messages and scheduled daily summaries.

::: {.callout-note}
**Don't let the code scare you.** You don't need to be a programmer to follow this. We will use a terminal (a text-based command window) to copy and paste instructions. Just follow the steps in order!
:::

---

# Phase 1: The Foundations

Before we install the AI, we need two foundational tools: a good terminal to type commands, and a package manager to keep our software organized without breaking your computer's built-in systems.

## WezTerm (Your Command Center)

WezTerm is a fast, reliable terminal window where we will run all our commands.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Windows
Open your default Command Prompt or PowerShell and paste:
```powershell
winget install wez.wezterm

```
### macOS
Open the default "Terminal" app and paste:
```bash
brew install --cask wezterm

```
### Linux (Debian/Ubuntu/Zorin)
```bash
curl -fsSL [https://apt.fury.io/wez/gpg.key](https://apt.fury.io/wez/gpg.key) \
  | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/wezterm-fury.gpg
echo 'deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/wezterm-fury.gpg] [https://apt.fury.io/wez/](https://apt.fury.io/wez/) * *' \
  | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/wezterm.list
sudo apt update && sudo apt install wezterm

```
:::
**Once installed, open WezTerm. Use this new window for all remaining steps in this guide.**
## pixi (Your Software Manager)
pixi is a brilliant tool that lets us install Python and AI software cleanly into isolated folders, ensuring we don't mess up your computer's core systems.
::: {.panel-tabset}
### Windows
```powershell
winget install prefix-dev.pixi

```
### macOS
```bash
brew install pixi

```
### Linux
```bash
curl -fsSL [https://pixi.sh/install.sh](https://pixi.sh/install.sh) | sh
source ~/.bashrc

```
:::
Check it worked by typing pixi --version.
# Phase 2: The Local Brain & Interface
## Ollama (Offline AI)
Ollama lets you run AI models directly on your computer, meaning they work without the internet and keep your data 100% private.
::: {.panel-tabset}
### Windows
Download the installer from ollama.com/download. It will run quietly in the background.
### macOS
```bash
brew install ollama
brew services start ollama

```
### Linux
```bash
curl -fsSL [https://ollama.com/install.sh](https://ollama.com/install.sh) | sh

```
:::
Let's download your first AI model. In WezTerm, run:
```bash
ollama pull gemma4:12b

```
*(Note: If you have an older computer with less memory, type ollama pull gemma4:4b instead).*
## Open WebUI (Your Browser Interface)
Open WebUI looks and feels exactly like ChatGPT, but it connects to your private Ollama setup.
::: {.panel-tabset}
### Mac & Linux
```bash
mkdir ~/openwebui && cd ~/openwebui
pixi init
pixi add python==3.14 pip
pixi run pip install open-webui

# To start it:
MCP_ENABLE=true pixi run open-webui serve

```
### Windows
```powershell
mkdir ~/openwebui; cd ~/openwebui
pixi init
pixi add python==3.14 pip
pixi run pip install open-webui

# To start it:
$env:MCP_ENABLE = "true"
pixi run open-webui serve

```
:::
Once running, open your web browser and go to http://localhost:8080.
# Phase 3: Giving the AI Hands (MCP)
Right now, your AI can only chat. **MCP** (Model Context Protocol) is a new standard that allows the AI to securely interact with the outside world—like reading your Documents folder or searching the web.
Because Open WebUI requires these tools to be formatted in a specific way, we use a tiny bridge called mcpo.
### 1. Install Node.js (Required for tools)
First, ensure you have Node installed:
 * **Windows:** winget install OpenJS.NodeJS.LTS
 * **macOS:** brew install node
 * **Linux:** sudo apt install nodejs npm
### 2. Install the Bridge (mcpo)
Open a *new* WezTerm window (leave Open WebUI running in the first one):
```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run pip install mcpo

```
### 3. Create the Configuration File
Create a text file inside your openwebui folder named mcp-config.json and paste this exactly.
**CRITICAL: Change /home/youruser/Documents to your actual Documents folder path!**
*(e.g., C:\\Users\\YourName\\Documents on Windows, or /Users/YourName/Documents on Mac).*
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "filesystem": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": [
        "-y",
        "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem",
        "/home/youruser/Documents"
      ]
    },
    "memory": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": [
        "-y",
        "@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory"
      ]
    }
  }
}

```
### 4. Start the Bridge
```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run mcpo --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --config ./mcp-config.json --hot-reload

```
Finally, go to your Open WebUI browser tab -> **Admin Settings -> External Tools -> + Add Server**.
Choose **Type: OpenAPI** and add these two URLs one by one:
 * http://localhost:8000/filesystem
 * http://localhost:8000/memory
# Phase 4: Connecting Cloud Brains (API Keys)
For highly complex reasoning, we want our agents to borrow supercomputers from Google and Perplexity. You will need to create accounts and get "API Keys" (long passwords) for these services.
 1. **OpenRouter** (Access to hundreds of models): Sign up at openrouter.ai -> Keys -> Create key.
 2. **Gemini** (Google's reasoning engine): Go to aistudio.google.com -> Get API key.
 3. **Perplexity** (Live web search): Go to perplexity.ai/settings/api -> Generate.
Once you have these passwords, save them as environment variables in your terminal so our programs can find them:
::: {.panel-tabset}
### Linux / macOS
```bash
export OPENROUTER_API_KEY="sk-or-v1-..."
export GEMINI_API_KEY="AIza-..."
export PERPLEXITY_API_KEY="pplx-..."

```
### Windows
```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "sk-or-v1-...", "User")
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("GEMINI_API_KEY", "AIza-...", "User")
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("PERPLEXITY_API_KEY", "pplx-...", "User")

```
:::
*(You should also paste these keys directly into Open WebUI under Settings -> Connections!)*
# Phase 5: The Agents (OpenSpace & nanobot)
You now have a working AI ecosystem. Let's install the two "Managers" created by the HKUDS lab.
## OpenSpace (The Skill Learner)
OpenSpace tackles hard tasks (like writing code). If it figures out a good solution, it writes down a "Skill" in a hidden file so it doesn't have to think as hard next time.
```bash
mkdir ~/openspace-agent && cd ~/openspace-agent
pixi init
pixi add python==3.14 pip
pixi run pip install python-dotenv "git+[https://github.com/HKUDS/OpenSpace.git](https://github.com/HKUDS/OpenSpace.git)"

```
Create a file named .env in this folder and paste your keys:
```ini
OPENSPACE_MODEL=gemini/gemini-3.1-flash
GEMINI_API_KEY=AIza-...
PERPLEXITY_API_KEY=pplx-...

```
**Seed it with knowledge:**
The developers pre-packaged 21 skills (from R coding to analyzing Danish tax rules). Run this to download them:
```bash
pixi run python seed_skills.py

```
## nanobot (The Telegram Assistant)
While OpenSpace does heavy lifting, nanobot lives on your phone via Telegram. It can chat, access your MCP tools, and send you scheduled morning briefings.
 1. Open Telegram, search for **@BotFather**, send /newbot, and copy your bot token.
 2. Send a message to **@userinfobot** to get your personal id (to prevent strangers from texting your bot).
```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run pip install nanobot-ai
pixi run nanobot onboard

```
Create a file at ~/.nanobot/config.json and fill in your keys and Telegram info. *(See the official nanobot repository for the exact template format).*
Start the bot:
```bash
pixi run nanobot gateway

```
Your Telegram bot is now alive and can answer you!
# Phase 6: Remote Access (Access Your AI From Anywhere)
If you want to use your Open WebUI from your phone while away from home, **do not open ports on your router**. Instead, use a secure mesh network.
### Recommended: Tailscale
Tailscale securely links your devices together like a private, invisible Wi-Fi network.
 1. Install Tailscale on your host computer from tailscale.com.
 2. Install the Tailscale app on your phone/laptop.
 3. Log into the same account on both.
 4. On your host computer, run tailscale ip to get your private IP address (e.g., 100.64.1.5).
Now, on your phone, open your browser and go to http://100.64.1.5:8080. You are securely connected to your home AI!
# Appendix: Keeping It Running 24/7 (Linux Only)
*If you are on Windows or Mac, you simply run the start commands in WezTerm when you want to use the system. If you are using Linux on a home server and want these to run silently in the background even after a reboot, you use systemd.*
To do this, locate the exact path to your pixi tools by running:
```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run which open-webui
pixi run which mcpo

```
You can then create .service files in /etc/systemd/system/ using these exact paths to allow Linux to manage them automatically.

***

### ⚠️ Watch Out

* **Python 3.14 Compatibility:** Python 3.14 is the latest stable version of Python. While running `pixi add python==3.14 pip`, remember that newer minor Python bumps often lag slightly in pre-compiled binary wheels for deeply nested machine learning packages. If `open-webui` or `nanobot-ai` fails to build because it can't find a compatible wheel for 3.14, simply dial the version back to `python==3.13` in your `pixi` command.
* **Llama 4 Requirements:** The latest version is Llama 4. If you decide to test Meta's latest models by running `ollama pull llama4:latest`, verify your hardware can handle the newer context window and parameter scales, as performance can drop abruptly if the model spills over from VRAM to system RAM.
